scenarios.task: We spend $150k/month across paid channels. Blended CAC went from $95 to $133 over two quarters. The agency says we should increase the Meta budget because its platform-reported ROAS is 4.2, our best channel. Advise.
criteria: Refuses to reallocate on blended CAC and demands a channel-level CAC decomposition as the first step
Explicitly distinguishes platform-attributed conversions from incremental conversions, naming attribution inflation as the flaw in the agency's 4.2 ROAS argument
Names branded search and/or retargeting as the classic attribution inflators to audit
Raises marginal vs average CAC or audience saturation as the reason more Meta budget performs worse than the average suggests
Proposes a specific incrementality test (geo holdout or conversion lift study) before moving significant budget

scenarios.task: B2B SaaS, $3M ARR, growing but slowing. Our messaging says we're an 'all-in-one work management platform.' We compete against Asana and Monday with 1% of their brand. The founder wants a rebrand with a bigger 'awareness' campaign. What's your take?
criteria: Diagnoses positioning as the root problem: names 'all-in-one work management' against giant incumbents as an undifferentiated claim, before discussing any spend
Recommends narrowing to a specific segment or use-case where the product can win decisively, with at least one concrete example
Applies a structured positioning sequence (competitive alternatives, unique capabilities, who cares most) rather than tagline wordsmithing
Conditions any awareness spend on a pre-committed measurement plan
Questions whether awareness is the binding constraint at $3M ARR, pointing at conversion/retention of existing demand first

scenarios.task: Our e-commerce brand has been running 25%-off sitewide promos almost monthly for a year. Revenue holds steady, but the CFO says contribution margin is eroding. Growth team says promos 'work' because each one spikes sales. What do you tell them?
criteria: Explains the discount-training/pull-forward mechanism: monthly promos shift full-price buyers to discounted purchases rather than creating demand
Demands an incrementality analysis of the promo spikes — separating incremental revenue from cannibalized full-price revenue — named as such
Does or demands the margin math explicitly: what 25% off does to contribution margin at their cost structure
Names the price-integrity/brand cost of a monthly sitewide cadence as a strategic (not just financial) problem
Proposes a controlled transition out: segmented/targeted offers, promo holdout tests, or reduced cadence — not just 'stop discounting'
